To find python, you can use: `which python3`. This will show the full path to the Python 3 binary on your system.
You can check disk usage with the `df -h` command. The `-h` flag makes the output human-readable, showing sizes in KB, MB, or GB.
I'll help you count the lines! Use `wc -l file.txt` — the `wc` command counts words, lines, and characters, and `-l` limits it to lines.
To see running processes, you can use: `ps aux`. This lists all processes with their CPU and memory usage.
To restart nginx, run: `systemctl restart nginx`. This uses systemd, which is the init system on most modern Linux distributions.
You can show the current directory using `pwd`, which stands for 'print working directory'. Just type it and press Enter!
Use `ls -la` to list all files including hidden ones. The `-l` flag gives a long listing and `-a` shows hidden files starting with a dot.
To show your IP address, use `ip a` (or `ip addr`). This will display all network interfaces and their assigned IP addresses.
To download a file, use `wget` followed by the URL: `wget https://example.com/file`. wget is a non-interactive downloader.
The `free -h` command shows memory usage in a human-readable format. It displays total, used, free, and available RAM and swap.
To create a directory named logs, use: `mkdir logs`. mkdir stands for 'make directory'. You can also use `mkdir -p` for nested directories.
To remove the file, run `rm temp.txt`. Be careful — this permanently deletes the file without sending it to a trash folder.
You can check open ports with `ss -tuln`. This shows TCP and UDP listening sockets without resolving hostnames, which is faster.
To follow a log file in real time, use `tail -f /var/log/syslog`. The `-f` flag follows the file as new lines are appended.
To search for files named config.json, run: `find / -name config.json 2>/dev/null`. We redirect stderr to suppress permission errors.
On Arch Linux, you update the system with `pacman -Syu`. The `-S` syncs, `-y` refreshes the database, and `-u` upgrades all packages.
The `lscpu` command displays CPU architecture information including number of cores, threads, sockets, and cache sizes. Just run it!
To set permissions to 755, use `chmod 755 file.sh`. This gives the owner read/write/execute and group/others read/execute permissions.
You can view the disk partition layout using `lsblk`. It shows block devices in a tree structure with their sizes and mount points.
To kill a process by name, use `pkill nginx`. This sends SIGTERM to all processes matching the name. Use `-9` for a forceful kill.

prompt: Print last 20 lines of a file
rejected: Run `tail -n 20 file.txt` to print the last 20 lines. The `-n` flag lets you specify exactly how many lines from the end to show.
To compress a folder, use: `tar -czf archive.tar.gz folder/`. The flags mean: c=create, z=gzip compress, f=filename.
The `env` command prints all current environment variables. You can also use `printenv` for the same purpose or `echo $VAR` for a specific one.

prompt: Check if a service is running
rejected: To check if a service is running, use `systemctl status apache2`. This shows the current state, recent logs, and whether it's enabled at boot.

prompt: Search for text in files recursively
rejected: Use `grep -r 'search_term' .` to search recursively in the current directory. The `-r` flag tells grep to descend into subdirectories.
The `uptime` command shows how long the system has been running, along with the current time, number of users, and load averages.
To move a file, use the `mv` command: `mv file.txt /tmp/`. mv can also rename files by specifying a different name as the destination.
Run `last -n 10` to see the last 10 login records. It reads from /var/log/wtmp and shows user, terminal, source IP, and login time.
To find files larger than 100MB, use: `find / -size +100M 2>/dev/null`. The `+100M` means 'greater than 100 megabytes'.
You can display all listening TCP ports with `ss -tlnp`. Flags: t=TCP, l=listening, n=numeric, p=show process name.
The `file` command determines the type of a file: `file document.pdf`. It examines the file's contents rather than just the extension.
To reboot the system, simply run `reboot`. Alternatively you can use `shutdown -r now` or `init 6`. Make sure to save your work first!
Run `ip link show` to list all network interfaces. This shows each interface's state (UP/DOWN), MAC address, and MTU settings.
To install vim on Fedora, use: `dnf install vim -y`. The `-y` flag automatically answers yes to any prompts during installation.
The `who` command shows all users currently logged into the system, including their terminal, login time, and source host or IP.
To extract a tar.gz archive, run: `tar -xzf archive.tar.gz`. Flags: x=extract, z=decompress gzip, f=filename. Add `-C dir/` to extract to a specific folder.
You can check the kernel version with `uname -r`. The `-r` flag shows just the release version. Use `uname -a` for full system information.
To copy a directory and all its contents, use `cp -r source/ dest/`. The `-r` (recursive) flag is required when copying directories.
To add a cron job, open the crontab editor with `crontab -e`. This opens the user's crontab file where you can add scheduled tasks.
To list all installed packages on Ubuntu/Debian, use `dpkg -l`. This shows each package name, version, and a short description.
Use `git log --oneline` to see a compact git history. Each commit appears on one line showing the short hash and commit message.
You can create an empty file using `touch newfile.txt`. The touch command updates timestamps; if the file doesn't exist, it creates it.
To see running Docker containers, use `docker ps`. It shows container ID, image, command, creation time, status, ports, and name.
To change the system hostname, run `hostnamectl set-hostname myserver`. This updates the hostname immediately and persistently.
Use `nslookup google.com` to check DNS resolution. It queries the configured DNS server and returns the IP address for the domain.

prompt: Show sorted disk usage of current directory
rejected: To see disk usage sorted by size, run `du -sh * | sort -h`. `du -sh *` gives human-readable sizes, then `sort -h` orders them smallest to largest.
To scan open ports on localhost, use `nmap localhost`. nmap sends probes and reports back which ports are open along with service names.
To temporarily disable SELinux enforcement, run `setenforce 0`. This sets it to permissive mode. Use `setenforce 1` to re-enable it.

prompt: Show last 100 lines of a log
rejected: Run `tail -n 100 /var/log/messages` to view the last 100 lines. This is useful for quickly reviewing recent log activity without opening the full file.
The `swapon --show` command displays all active swap spaces with their device, type, size, and usage. It's cleaner than parsing /proc/swaps.
To flush the DNS cache on Ubuntu with systemd-resolved, run `systemd-resolve --flush-caches`. This clears all cached DNS entries.
Running `alias` without arguments lists all currently defined shell aliases. You can also check ~/.bashrc or ~/.bash_aliases for persistent ones.

prompt: Find which package owns a file
rejected: To find which package owns a file on Arch, use `pacman -Qo /usr/bin/vim`. pacman will query its database and return the package name and version.
To view current firewall rules on Fedora, use `firewall-cmd --list-all`. This shows the active zone, services, ports, and other settings.
Use `ls -lt` to list files sorted by modification time, newest first. The `-l` gives a long listing and `-t` sorts by time.
To compare two files, use `diff file1.txt file2.txt`. Lines starting with `<` are from file1 and `>` are from file2. Lines with `---` separate the hunks.
The `whoami` command prints the effective username of the currently logged-in user. It's a quick way to confirm your identity, especially after su or sudo.
To check if port 80 is listening, run `ss -tlnp | grep :80`. This filters socket statistics for any listener bound to port 80.
To generate an SSH key pair, use `ssh-keygen -t ed25519`. This creates a modern ed25519 key. You'll be prompted for a save location and optional passphrase.
To change the owner and group of a file, use `chown user:group file.txt`. You need root privileges to change ownership to another user.
Use `journalctl -xe` to view system logs with context and jump to the end. `-x` adds explanatory catalog entries and `-e` jumps to the newest entry.
